speaker: interviewer
text: --Waisman, tape 5.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so then in the winter of-- of '44, sometime in that time, as the Russians were advancing we again got packed up in cattle trains and destination unknown to us. And for days, we seemed to be in-- in these cars, cold, and hungry, and worried. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] People were in a weakened condition. A lot of them were dying in front of us. One incident that I remember, I remember being huddled in-- in-- in a corner, and I found a little crack. And-- and what I remember from this trip on the outside is that I was looking out, I saw a farmhouse in the distance, and I remember smoke coming out of the chimney. And I remember a small figure with a bucket going from the farmhouse to the well to fetch some water. And I followed this figure. I don't know, it looked like a youngster. I don't know if it was a boy or a girl. And I followed them to the well, and I followed them back to the farm. And I almost transformed myself into that person. And I visualized what's happening inside. I visualized food. I visualized parents. I visualized, you know, happiness as I had known it at home. And I was very envious of this figure. That's all I remember from that trip. The other memorable things in there is that you could hear conversation, but it all didn't make sense. We were sort of like in a twilight zone. Somebody-- they made a little round, and somebody, somehow, don't ask me how, had a can of meat. And they build a fire inside with I don't know what. They must have had something there. And they were warming up this can of of meat. And they didn't punch a hole in it, and the thing exploded. And I was in the corner, because I've moved up to the fire to warm myself up a little bit. And when this exploded, it exploded on my face, and I had my eyes closed. I was in excruciating pain because I had these burns. When I'm tired, you can still see it on my-- in here and in here. But it all healed, and I'll tell you how it healed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because when they opened the doors-- you know, and we were stepping over bodies. Half of us were still alive. The rest were all dead. And somebody said, you're in Buchenwald. I never heard of Buchenwald. And I asked, what's-- what's Buchenwald? And somebody said, it's a political camp. And I says, what's political? And a guy behind me says, tell them you're political. I says, well, why? He says, because it's better than being Jewish. You'll live at least two weeks longer. I remember thinking, is this guy nuts? No. In what language were you speaking?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Yiddish. We were speaking Polish and Yiddish or-- or German. You can hear all these languages. Now, so when we got in and we undressed and went through the showers, before going to the showers-- or no, after the showers-- There was this huge-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it looked like-- like a pool, but much smaller than-- than-- than a regular pool, a swimming pool. And everybody had to go through there, and it was sort of a white substance. It-- it-- it was some sort of a disinfectant. And people were going in there, dunking into this, and out they came. And it was-- I think it was a disinfectant. So of course, I had all these-- all this-- my face, I couldn't see out of my eyes. And I remember thinking, when they take one look at me with all-- all the-- these huge openings, the sores on my-- on my face, I'll be marked for dead immediately. And really-- I thought that this is going to really, after all these years, and this is getting close, I'm going to make it. And I thought, I won't survive that. There was a Gestapo man with a stick. And as I went right up to the neck, and he pushed me right on there, just completely down, you know. But I came out, I-- I-- I just had to do this, and it hurt like hell. And this thing just-- I thought I was going to die right then and there. But what I didn't know is that it was a disinfectant, and the sores healed in no time afterwards, and I was fine. It was just a miracle. I went into Block 8, which, again, was political, truly political, and there were very few Jews. Another friend of mine that is on that picture I showed you, Abraham Chopnik, we shared a bunk together and became very, very close friends. He lives in New York now. And I think he was telling me that he was going to do this as well. And then my life in Buchenwald was actually the easiest on me from all the camps. Yet [INAUDIBLE] and all these other ones are not known that much. But the-- the agony there was just horrendous. Unbeknownst to me, 50 years later, there's a book that came out in French, the one that I showed you, where some of the prisoners, some German, some Russians, and some other nationalities that were in there that were sort of the leadership of the camp, they were political prisoners. One of them, for instance, we called him Willie, was a 5-foot little German, who, in 1938 said, Mr. Hitler, I don't agree with Nazism. So they threw him into camp rather than killing him. And he was instrumental in one-- at one occasion when-- when we were marched out before April 1945, a few days earlier. They were trying to get all the Jews out there to kill them. And we were standing on-- on-- on a-- on a pell to be counted. And this Gestapo man, with his stick, you know, banging, and his shoes, walked over and he says, [GERMAN]. All Jews step forward. And Willie, he sort of glanced at both Chopnik and myself and winked at us. And he says to him, Ich haben nicht keine Juden. I don't have any Jews here. And he knew we were Jewish. So Willie that day saved our lives, because those that were marched out, most of them didn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that Buchenwald wasn't as bad as the other camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because some of these kids-- some of these elders, you know-- unbeknownst to me, this book came out-- and they took it upon themselves to do everything possible to shelter these children. Over-- some close to 3,000 children went through Buchenwald. And for all these years, they thought that they failed. And it-- and it wasn't known. So they didn't brag about it. But they managed to save a thousand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Suddenly I said to myself, why are things so good for us in here, to Chopnik and I? When it came time to work, they left us alone. Did you not work in Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Buchenwald, we didn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We weren't there very long.

speaker: interviewer
text: --spend your day?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were sitting there. They told us to hide. We were hiding in the daytime. So we made ourselves scarce. But these men were responsible. They were hiding us. And here I thought, hey, we're heroes. We are making it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there enough food in Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't work, so we didn't exert as much energy. So the piece of bread and the soup was quite sufficient. And one day we said, we have care parcels, once I remember. And again, I found out-- 50 years later I found out that the leaders there apparently received some care parcels from the Red Cross. The reason they received them also is that they wanted to-- they were-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: The reason they received the parcels?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is that the-- the Red Cross was inspecting the camp, and they wanted to show that they were giving these out. So we had-- so suddenly I find myself with a-- a-- a piece of chocolate. We didn't-- for  a while, we didn't even know what it was. It was incredible. So we-- we were-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was unbelievable. We had-- what else was there? There was a chocolate bar, and there were some other little goodies that-- that were there. And so then, of course, we were getting close to-- we-- we heard rumors that the Americans were advancing, and that we could conceivably make it. The-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Gestapo, again, tried to get as many Jews as they possibly can. And there were also rumors going around the camp that you might as well go out when they ask you to go and-- and-- and go to another camp. Because they said, we're going to take you to another camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some thought, maybe there's a better chance to go to another camp. Because the rumor was that the camp is set up full of dynamite, and they're going to explode the-- the camp with the prisoners in it. They didn't want to-- the Allies to discover all the horrors that they had committed. So I remember lying on the floor with Chopnik, and let's escape. And we knew that very few ever escaped successfully. Machine gun flying overhead, and we're lying down there. And they  said, well, maybe we'll be liberated. Like hell we're going to get liberated. It's going to explode. We're not going to make it. What would have happened had you tried to escape and you'd have been caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we might have-- we would have been killed instantly. The decision was made for us, because there was suddenly a hush that came over the camp. And-- [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so here we were, Chopnik, and I, and trying to make up our mind. Do we stick it out in here and chances of making it, or do we try and escape? The decision was made for us, because all of a sudden there was dead silence, quiet. The shelling stopped. Our block, Block 8, was close to the main gate, where we every day looked at it. And there was a clock there, and Arbeit macht frei. All these goodies were-- were there on the entrance.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember pulling myself and looking out. And I remember the time. It was 10 minutes to 4:00. And if you speak to Chopnik, he has a different kind of memories. You know, each one remembers something differently. And I saw soldiers coming in through the gate. And I thought to myself, I looked to Chopnik, and I said, hey, we're not blown up. I think we're free. These are not Germans. Have a look! Chapnick took a look. He says, you're right.

speaker: interviewer
text: We ran out of the-- of the barracks. People everywhere started running and milling around. There were people coming in on jeeps. Soldiers marching. I remember going out of the-- of the compound, just to make sure that on the-- what's on the other side. It was April 11, 1945, 10 minutes to 4:00. There were daffodils coming out or trying to come out. And I remember thinking, how could anything grow in that godforsaken place? Everything was just new. I'll be free. I ran back in. I ran after a jeep. And there was a guy that was stood up, and he says, Ich bin [GERMAN]. Ich bin [GERMAN]. Ich bin [GERMAN]. A rabbi, I thought to myself? He hasn't got a beard. He has a uniform. This is crazy. This is a dream. This is not happening. I saw two black soldiers. There were more coming. And I said, Americans? Black? What kind of people? They're black people? That's-- that's weird. That's strange.

speaker: interviewee
text: I ran up to touch this one guy. And he pushed me aside because I still had some scabs on my face, smelly probably. And he just pushed me aside. But I took a look at his face, and it was ingrained in my memory. And 30 years later, I saw that same face in the And we phoned the War Department, and I found out who he was, and we are reunited. But that's another story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon Bass, Dr. Leon Bass. He went back after and became a teacher, became a principal of a school, got his PhD, and on and  on  he went. And now we spoke to-- to students about our experiences. And it-- he's a wonderful human being. We've become very, very good friends. But going back to the liberation. The soldiers that came in, the rabbi that came in, they weren't prepared for what they found in the camps. Nobody told them about it. I remember grabbing a can of pork and beans. I wanted to eat it because I was hungry. And a man knocked it out of me, and I wanted to hit him. I couldn't reach him because he was a tall man, and I wasn't-- I was 14. My growth was stunted. I wasn't very tall. But I still wanted to-- I found out that he was a doctor, and he saved my life again. Because a lot of people ate this pork and beans, and our stomachs were shrunk. We couldn't digest rich food. People were dying of dysentery. And-- and-- and the Red Cross came a few days later, and they gave us baby food, which was more to-- to what we could eat. And after a while, we left the barracks, and went to-- we occupied the SS barracks. Much better.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you realized that the war was over and you were liberated, what did liberation mean to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were so many-- you know, for days, we were-- I don't think I saw Chopnik for two days, because he was running around in the camp speaking to people. We slept wherever we could. It didn't matter. We ate where we-- so each-- although we-- we went through the same thing, we see each other now and then, and we reminisce about things. Completely different stories, because we didn't share.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the liberation, it was my own thoughts, or my first thought was, I wanted to go home, you know? It's enough. And we were all euphoric. We were walking on air that we survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, you know, the news started trickling in about the people that died, and how many died, and so on. And it was unbelievable. We didn't believe the enormity of the deed. Millions and millions had died. You lived in your-- in your own small world, and you just went day by day, and you didn't see the big picture. You had blinkers on your eyes, and you only saw what's in front of you. And so as time went by, you realized the enormity of it all. We were in Buchenwald for another three months, because all of us wanted to leave. And then there were the Americans and some people that they designated to-- to look after us were saying, look, you can't leave in here because it's a different world. There's no home there. Give us a chance to find-- to find who is alive and who isn't. It was complete chaos, what was going on. And some of the eldest among us came and started looking after us. Suddenly-- suddenly there was a group of us that started getting childhood diseases. Like mumps broke out. In the camp years, we-- we didn't have time to be sick. And suddenly we had doctors looking after us, taking our blood pressure, giving us special foods, giving us-- and taking everybody's history, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were wandering around in-- in-- in the neighborhood. And for the longest time, we wouldn't venture out, because you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when you're enclosed, your mentality is such that you don't believe it. I remember touching jeeps when they first came in. And these soldiers looked at us, it's-- what's the matter with them? They're crazy?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't believe for days that-- that-- that it's true. I remember going to sleep and waking up, and I had to pinch myself. I'm-- I'm free. We don't have to do to stand for hours to be counted. We don't have to worry about being shot. We don't have to--

speaker: interviewer
text: All these things that happen to you for all these years, suddenly you have to deal with each one and process all the thinking, that you're free. It's not easy. You know what I mean? It-- it-- it's a whole process of trying to assimilate the things around you. Most difficult. And of course, when we were de-humanized there, you didn't act like a human being either.

speaker: interviewee
text: Suddenly you have these people. In fact, [NON-ENGLISH], who's-- who's now the chief rabbi of Israel, his brother, [NON-ENGLISH] spoke English, so he was sort of the-- the liaison. He also filled out our vital statistics, where we're from, in English, because, you know, the Americans wanted to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everything, everything was new to us. We didn't have any normal feelings. We couldn't experience, we-- hunger and thirst were about the only ones. And so we had this process. We were kind of wild, and we didn't listen to anybody because we had to come back to humanity and learn.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, of course, I didn't understand all of this. But since then, you know, there have been books written about the children in Buchenwald. Because psychologists and psychiatrists who examined us said, forget about these kids. They will not be able to become normal human beings. They have seen too much. Write them off. Don't try to rehabilitate them. They'll just live out their own lives, if they will  live out their own lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember reading about this, and I am happy to tell you that the experts were totally wrong. Because among this group was Elie Wiesel. Among this group was [NON-ENGLISH], who became-- who's now the chief rabbi  of Israel. Among this group were many, many doctors and lawyers now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Among this group-- with the exception of one friend that I have who is in an insane asylum in Ponoka, Alberta, Jack, who couldn't come to terms and couldn't switch from that life to a new life. It was too much for him, and he went insane. For a while, we used to go and visit him, and then I stopped because he didn't know me. We all were able to make a life for ourselves, to get married, and have children, and make contributions in our respective communities. So, so much for the experts. Human resiliency is totally amazing. I don't have a word for it. It's amazing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you start to put your life back together again?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were three months in-- in Buchenwald. Nowhere to go. A-- a journalist from Paris heard that there was a bunch of kids in Buchenwald. Kids? He came to see. He went back and wrote an article. I think it was or the two papers, both carried a banner, J'Accuse. I accuse the governments of the world for a bunch of kids that survived, and nobody is willing to open their doors. They brought the phrase, J'Accuse, you know, from the Dreyfus case that Emile Zola wrote way back [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] about-- you know, you're familiar with the Dreyfus case. Anyway, when this article came out, France said, we will accept as many as want to come to France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the journalist? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. I can probably find out for you. So off we went to France, and we landed in a place called Ecouis, which is in-- in Normandy. From there, we regrouped those that wanted to continue on the education in-- in the Jewish seminars, they could, and those that didn't. And so they sort of more or less categorized and tried to bring us back to humanity. Again, I remember the first dormitory. We-- we put-- we threw all the beds down and lit a match to it. We just couldn't understand. And-- and they had experts come to talk to us. We just couldn't be regimented to anything. We were angry. We were angry at the world. We were angry that so many died. We were angry we didn't have a home to go to. We were angry.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out about the fate of your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that she went to Treblinka. And until I saw the and I saw Lanzmann's description of-- just the train going to Treblinka, I-- it just brought me to pieces, because I realized this is where my beloved Mum that I adored went. This is where her last days were, is on-- on the way to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you-- sorry. When were you reunited with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some six months after. I saw her name on the-- on   the Red Cross list. And also, when people were traveling in Europe-- you know, we-- I had a little picture of myself and all the statistics-- who I am, where I was born. And, you know, as you give a business card, we were giving these pictures out. And she knew that I was alive by seeing a picture of me, but she didn't know where I was. She knew somewhere in France. So I found her before she found me. Now, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember the group that Elie Wiesel was, they wanted to have kosher food, and so they were separated. And I remember him saying that, oh, sure we doubt God, but I'm going to study and find out what it's all about. The rest of us-- there was somebody that we used to call Stalin, you know. It was-- at that time, you know, Russia was in everybody's good books. So he was very socialistic-minded, you know, and he sort of talked all of us into believing that there is no God. And he says, I'll show you guys what we should do. He was a little older. And so there were these different groups. The Poles stuck together. The Hungarians stuck together. And-- and they started separating us.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to go to break.
